instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Gender Equality Starts at Home
Let’s date back to the 70s and 80s. The majority of Indian households back then had women taking care of the home while the men earned the livelihood. The society believed in gender stereotypes, and from early childhood, girls and boys were treated differently. Gender equality for children thus remained a far cry.
Fifty years later, did India change? Let’s look at some statistics. Joint research by the International Institute of Population Sciences (IIPS) and Tata Institute of Social Sciences (TISS) claims Indian women contribute ten times more than the men in unpaid household work and caregiving[1]. The GER, UIDSE 2021-22 report says only 3 in 5 girls get enrolled in higher education while the rest are gripped by domestic chores, child marriages, and other social norms[2]. Thus, while gender equality for children still paints a grim picture, adult women are no less deprived.
What about the workspace? A recent ruling by the Supreme Court of India says a two-year childcare leave along with the maternity leave of 180 days is a constitutional mandate for women. However, data shows 73% of women leave their jobs post-childbirth, 50% quit to look after their children, 48% leave after rejoining, and 30% suffer from pay cuts after maternity leaves[3]. Coming to salaries too, there’s still a prominent gender pay gap. According to the World Economic Forum’s recent report, against every Rs 100 earned by Indian men, women earn Rs 40, taking India to the 129th position among 146 countries in the Global Gender Gap Index[4].
In India, the concept of gender inequality is deep-rooted in the society’s mindset and norms, visible at home, in the workplace, and in public places alike[5]. To eradicate the gender gap in the future, how about raising your children in a different environment?
Here’s what you can do to make sure that gender equality starts at home.
Sharing the work: The Covid-19 pandemic saw Indian families share domestic chores between men and women. Carry forward the practice and divide the household work not just between the husband and wife, but among the children too. Let gender equality for children remain a constant choice[6].
Empower your children: Talk to your child about gender gaps and women’s rights. Explain to them how gender equality right from childhood can make the world a better place. This can teach your son to value women and their rights equally as he grows up, while your daughter can feel more confident[6].
Breaking stereotypes: Let your children understand that ideas like ‘Men don’t cry’ or ‘Mothers should cook’ are nothing but stereotypes and conditioning imposed by a patriarchal society. Researchers say, boys and girls start believing in these stereotypes within the age bracket of 3-10 years. Hence, early introductions of gender equality for your children can be helpful[6].
Putting a stop to body shaming: Dictated by the society, a lot of people believe in beauty standards, which can hinder gender equality for children too. There’s an overwhelming practice to judge people by their looks and body shapes and taunt those who don’t fit the benchmarks. Make your children aware of how body shaming harms a person and that beauty lies in the mind and behaviour. However, remember to set an example as parents[6].
Listen to your child: Today’s youth are far more informed and outspoken about rights. Think of how Malala Yousafzai stood tall for child education and Greta Thunberg called out world leaders for climate action. Trust these positives, and change your views if needed to make the world a better place, ensuring gender equalities for children[6].
Let’s make this world a gender-equal land together!
References:
[1] https://www.newindianexpress.com/cities/delhi/2024/Mar/24/indian-women-do-domestic-chores-10x-than-men#:~:text=Delhi-,'Indian%20women%20do%20domestic%20chores%2010x%20than%20men',98%20minutes%20to%20the%20same
[2] https://www.cry.org/blog/gender-inequality-in-indian-education/
[3] https://thebetterindia.com/349558/chennai-sankari-sudhar-job-portal-for-new-mothers-women-with-career-break-overqualified-housewives/
[4] https://www.businesstoday.in/latest/economy/story/india-on-global-gender-gap-index-disparity-continues-as-women-early-only-rs-40-for-every-rs-100-earned-by-men-433190-2024-06-13
[5] https://www.hindustantimes.com/ht-insight/gender-equality-in-india-progress-challenges-and-solutions-101730457778734.html
[6] https://medium.com/we-the-peoples/gender-equality-starts-at-home-seven-tips-for-raising-feminist-kids-75e1bf00b863
[7] https://www.unwomen.org/en/news-stories/feature-story/2023/05/gender-equality-starts-at-home-five-ways-to-drive-change-within-the-family
Disclaimer:
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. metadata.title: Gender equality starts in the home
